item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Most of what I had to say was already said by Buttfranklin 3 years ago, so downvote, but that does include that, despite not being an entry I liked it was definitely a promising author and a very good first concluded attempt.

So, I do want to know what iznaroth have been doing and if we have new articles from him.

item.title: Found something whilst translating this
item.content:encoded:
Hey, i'm currently working on tranlsating this into -DE, and discovered that there are significant parts of the Exploration log not displayed because they look like this:
>**Cap:** Entryway is regular. No sign of anomalous activity. Hume levels appear regular.

Is this intended? They are not visible in the article because of the space missing! Should i change it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I find the censoring of the survival rate to be unnecesary.
Other than that, this is certainly a very nice article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think I read an earlier version of this way back during the 3000 contest, but there's a couple things that either weren't there or I didn't catch before that I can't help but love.

The dimensions of the entity being extremely tall and extremely thin are super creepypasta-ish, but it's super fitting given the perfectly campy premise.

And then, when you actually get into their world, and they're not even hostile there? It's so appropriately Monsters Inc.

I love goofy little details that complement the horror like that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My favorite part: how i was SURE this was a SCP is paranoid about a totally normal thing…maybe there is no anomaly…maybe the paranoia is the anomaly"

a couple jarring things made that unlikely. then NOPE ITS REAL!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
How did I miss this during the contest? Though I'll agree the logs were a bit stilted on a couple occasions, this actually spooked me pretty well.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow, this was good, for me at least. I like the ways reality changes throughout the page, nice touch. Oh, and having it use my username at the end? Awesome, I was not expecting 4th wall breaks, but here they were.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So wait. Is the MTF Eight Blind Men or Three Blind Men?

And seems like effects of this scip include retrocausality?

item.title: No vote for now
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, I don't often get that creepy feeling while reading articles on this site, but your SCP certainly raised a few hairs. Probably because I fit most of the criteria for an ideal victim :/

That being said, it's a no vote for now. Fix some of the issues previous commenters have mentioned, and I'll hand deliver you my upvote.

item.title: On The Last Bit
item.content:encoded: I don't understand that last part. Not the .truth unedited file, but the part that talks about someone trying to access the Editor Program to change the unedited document. Is it implying that the creatures are trying to edit his .truth file to hide the information, or that the author of the .truth file is trying to access the computer again to add more but they blocked him out?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I enjoyed this concept and I though it was well executed. The idea of something watching you as an actual entity is interesting. The eight blind men segment was really cool, and for a second there I thought that the foundation being aware of the vanishing of several members (Three Bind Men) ruined the tension, until I remembered that the effects don't destroy written accounts. Clever stuff. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
then onto some re-writing of the addenda

Be sure to announce it, I'll be back to reread and possibly rerate. This has beyond a doubt some of the most evocative SCProcedures I've seen here. I lost interest as it went on, and swapped to a Novote from my initial enthusiastic +1 from the excellently-crafted opening.

Looking forward to the edits.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for everyone that read and voted during the contest!

This was pretty much a random thought in the first place - I had something old that I wrote (the original draft was from 2012 if I remember correctly) that was kinda scary, saw the contest, and kinda said "Sure, let's do it, what the hell", expecting it to get last place, deleted, or a low rating. So for a first crack, this exceeded my expectations, and I learned a lot about my own writing. Gonna try to get a good image with actual rights up within the next few days, then onto some re-writing of the addenda. Again, thanks to everyone, the positive response was great and I got more useful feedback than I really know what to do with now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wasn't sure until the last collapsible. Fantastic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This one started good, then I very quickly began to feel it was a similar play on SCP-966s deal. But it boosted back up and had a pretty cool ending.

Solid article, though it can use some editing for flow. Great work.

item.title: Re: Staf Post: Open
item.content:encoded: Whoops, thought that website was a stock photo website, the URL was misleading. Image removed. I'll look for one with suitable permissions when the contest is over.

item.title: Staf Post: Open
item.content:encoded: Images used in posted articles must comply with the site's image policy. A source must be provided on the discussion page and the source must be compatible with the site's license. The author will be notified after this posting via Wikidot PM and will have 48 hours to properly source the image before it is edited out of the page. If the images are not wholly your own, please post the image's source as a link (or links) in reply to this post and edit it into your first post in this discussion thread.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Nine days after containment. Subject has fashioned a makeshift suit covered in light fixtures with a portable crank-generator. Noted that generator must be removed upon Subject's termination."

Unless this D-class is a miracle electrician, i don't think this is plausible. How do you even wear a light fixture suit? Where did this person get a portable crank-generator? Why was it there in the first place? The loose ends caught my downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I initially liked that the opening was well-crafted, then felt disappointed by the SCP, and finally liked the reveal, which I didn't initially expect. Though I was still iffy on the actual writing of the logs.

This isn't working for me quite yet, but I think it will work really well with some edits.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This gave me an amazing semi lucid nightmare.

My only criticism is the use of the users name in the page as others have mentioned.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I know we're talking edits for after the contest here, but yeah, it's still (as of edit 31) kinda iffy.

+ show block
– hide block

Think about it this way: the violence works on two levels. There's the weird/unexpected/gross factor, and then there's the fact that it serves to communicate info about these critters. We can infer from the rest of the article that they follow you but don't like being noticed, or noticing them breaks a "rule," and the sheer violence of their response supports that. The fact that the procedure is so perfectly described and uniform (exactly 117.2 sec, they always do the skin thing etc) suggests that either they are creatures of unchanging habit or inflexible rule-following, or that their response is a ritual intended to accomplish a specific task or send a specific message. The bone-carving and shaping remains into a pattern support these ideas.

So on the information end, what are they trying to accomplish? All I've got is that maybe the bone carving and such is a ritual that results in the erasure. I only got that idea just now, like 5 days later, so it's not exactly front and center. That, or this is just "what they do."

On the gross-out/weird end, [DATA EXPUNGED] and [REDACTED] (when used well) are generally placed so that the reader already had a good idea of what's in the box, but leaving them to work out the horrific details. You're already saying the gross stuff out loud, so the expungements aren't doing you much good.

So, basically, it's not hitting either purpose hard enough. I'm not saying you need to 100% spell out the motivations of the beings (you shouldn't) or that there's a particular way I think you should use your redactions. I'm just saying pick your reveals and concealments in this section to maximize both of those factors.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Three Blind Mice and the final collapsible really got me. I like your writing, and look forward to what else you'll bring. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Another excellent 3000 entry.
A decent build-up, nice character establishing and the hook-line-sinker payoff.

And concerning the "horror-setup" of the contest:
I think this was also excellently done.
A quite gruesome atmosphere and my favourite - extremely powerful SCPs that alter reality, but not as a stereotypical super-villain (*Rubs hands and laughs*) but rather casually ("Three Blind Men"').

Keep up the good work! :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The username module was less for the creep factor and more for a clarity of the timeframe that the event was happening, but it tends to be overused, so I can see it seeming kind of cringy.

For me it tends to be immersion breaking; "sirpudding" isn't anybody who works at the Foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree with pretty much all your criticisms. It's certainly been noted and I'm cutting/butchering the addenda that have dialogue. I actually edited out a lot of the expungements but missed the deadline by ~2 minutes so I had to revert it, but I know at least that it was a bad decision.

The username module was less for the creep factor and more for a clarity of the timeframe that the event was happening, but it tends to be overused, so I can see it seeming kind of cringy. If you're talking about the last bit of 'dialogue', that's not written by the SCP. It's written by a human, but I dunno if I implied it clearly enough.

Thanks for reading.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It had a strong start. You take some take a generic horror monster and you deconstruct it, showing how the Foundation engineers containment to observe and discredit an omnipresent phenomenon.

But...
There's a real nose dive in quality.

Then you get the cop-out expungements.

And then the rather generic incident log.

And then the MTF team gets slaughtered.

And then the username module shows up.

And then the SCP haxx the database.

And then there's the "through the eyes of madness" bit.

The editing denial bit was great though, and reminded me what I liked about the article.

Unfortunately it also shows how badly the article deteriorates in quality.

Overall, it has a strong opening that builds intrigue and exemplifies the narrative strengths of the Foundation, but it ultimately becomes a string of eye-rolling cliches that do little for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Shit, whoops, I had a feeling I missed an "A", it was what I used to call them but since the addendum was the same I changed it.

I think this a minor edit and therefore permissible.

Also, what would be a good high-ranking member that's not O5?

This seems like a Senior Researcher kind of job to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Shit, whoops, I had a feeling I missed an "A", it was what I used to call them but since the addendum was the same I changed it. Also, what would be a good high-ranking member that's not O5?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The idea of containment procedures being "build an artificial haunted house and trap someone in it" is great.

Some questions/nitpicks:

16-40

Why this range? I certainly remember being creeped out like this before 16 and, well, as recently as I read your article (so a couple of minutes ago when I was still over 40)!

searching for instances of SCP-3000-A. Appears to discover something within Grand Hall.

What is -A? It doesn't seem defined elsewhere.

resident overseer

Most people seem to assume that the overseers are generally isolated from anomalous stuff and really this seems kind of low level besides.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not even halfway through the SCProcedures, and I've already wanted to upvote on three separate occasions, so I did. Looking forward to the rest!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It should be, 2208 EDT on the 10th is almost two hours before the deadline. You only missed it by two minutes with Rev28.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reverted, but I'm not sure if that was early enough. If it wasn't, let me know.

item.title: Staff Post - Closed
item.content:encoded: This entry has had major editing work done since the contest deadline. This is in violation of the contest rules. Please revert the article back to the last revision prior to the deadline. If this has not been rectified by the time the contest ends this entry will be disqualified.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1. Edit I planned to make but forgot when it hit midnight. Definitely on my list when the end rolls around. Something like "Mirrors made from a flexible, shock resistant reflective surface" might work.

2. The intention was that when the resident D-Class is as far away from any entrance as possible, a small team enters and attempts to replace any light fixtures. They drop out if the D-class gets too close, and there's extra lights in storage (mentioned in an addenda). I need to rewrite that bit, as I forgot to mention things like what happens if the D-Class catches them.

3. Yeah, that's something I missed. Probably stems from the fact that the dialogue is bad.

4. Whoops, I left light in there. I meant to word it as "not perceivable in any wavelength or format" or something along those lines. Dunno if the contest rules allow that until after the voting, though.

5. Yeah, that's clunky. It means that in half the cases of avoidant personalities, no reaction to SCP-3000 occurs, even if it should.

Thanks for reading.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show comments
- hide comments

The household has 32 windows and 46 mirrors.

What prevents the D-class from smashing up the mirrors?

The following rooms are to be lit: the Bedroom, West Hall, Grand Hall, Rec Room, and storage.

How are the lights to be replaced when they burn out? Are there special light fixtures that let bulbs be replaced much faster than with normal screw-fixtures? Or are new lightbulbs provided along with food, and the D-class "bait" does the replacements?

SCP-3000 sufferers can not sleep under any circumstances.

D-17729 seemed to be a lot more lucid than I'd expect for someone who hadn't slept for that long. Does the SCP make it so the victims don't need as much sleep?

SCP-3000-1 is not perceivable in any wavelength of light.

They didn't try using X-rays, sonar or ultrasound?

Simply put, about 50% of individuals with avoidant disorders do not react to anything in scenarios where SCP-3000-1 would manifest.

This needs to be made more clear; I have no idea what you mean.

The following text

Should be "the following transcript".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I forgot to say this earlier, but upvote for creating a skip that combines my top three phobias. X3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It certainly seems that the addenda are the weakest portion. In retrospect, it makes sense - I always felt stronger about this piece's description, and I've been iffy on my own dialogue since I started writing, but the actual criticism is nice, because now I have a real problem to work on fixing.

Thanks for reading and the feedback.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Eh. Not really feeling this.

There is a lot that I want to like here. The idea of a monster that thrives in the type of environments that creep people out is fantastic, and I really liked the description. Then I hit the second and third collapsibles and the article just kind of fizzled out. There is only so many times I can hear a D-class talk to themselves as they break down, before it kind of gets old and bland. Combine that with some narmy dialogue in the exploration log and all the tension you built with your description melts away.

-1. Nice description, but the addenda need a bit better execution.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
After a little internal debate, I'd say you're right, and I made an edit to show it. Feels a bit risky since it was done like 1 minute off the deadline, but whatever. I was iffy about redacting stuff, so I swiped a couple of chunks from the procedure on my notes and tried to stitch it in a way that left some stuff up for interpretation while still giving enough to say "Yeah, it's bad". If the change sucks, lemme know, though, I'm not sure if I went too far or not far enough.

Thanks for the feedback.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked a lot of this. The containment procedures being trapping someone in a haunted house was very neat.

I actually like the dictionary definition thing. One important thing to note from a psychology standpoint, antisocial personality disorder IS sociopathy. If you're looking for people who avoid social contact, that's avoidant personality disorder.

The real weakest part was the MTF exploration log. The dialogue with a couple of people disappearing was ok, and it's definitely interesting that they're immediately exploring a ruined site, but it really mostly just rehashed what happens in just about every MTF exploration log. Edit: Actually, you know, it's fine.

Other than that, all the DATA EXPUNGED violence is pretty cliche, as is it erasing people. I like the whole "murder after three times" thing because it really tied this back into making ghost stories/superstitions true thing. I just think it would be way more interesting to give a real answer that had some thought about the beings rather than "EXPUNGED carved bones EXPUNGED."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For the ending, I think it was definitely an open to interpretation thing. I initally thought it to be a sort of "if the d-class on the other end said goodbye, what's trying to make edits" thing, and you're the one that locks it (since I had no real way to allow you to input Y/N to my knowledge), but the idea that the editor PC is being locked by the entity is another way to look at it.

I'm definitely debating over what to do with log 2. It's pretty obviously bad, but I dunno if I wanna just totally cut it out, remove all the dialogue and just describe the scenario for a little clarity, or rewrite it more carefully.

For the sort of incoherent effects, the memory effect really was a plot device for the exploration log. The spatial anomaly was less so, because there's definitely an origin for it but it's not particularly original, so I'm thinking about rewriting it. I kind of feel like I'm running into a wall of what I want to change before the editing deadline versus what I want to just do after the voting's over. I'm a little hesitant to make a ton of major changes, but I'm still considering it.

Thanks for the feedback.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I had a more specific comment lined up, but Buttfranklin basically covered most of my points first, so eh.

I will say that this has a bunch of different effects, some of which (like the memory thing and it messing with space) seem to be there without rhyme or reason. I like the idea of something erasing people to cover its tracks, but there's very little indication of that in the article (then again, I suppose it'd be difficult for the Foundation to work that out).

Also, I'm not really sure how much the second log works at all. To me, at least, it doesn't do anything that a reader couldn't infer from the first addendum, and whilst that's not necessarily a killer, the dodgy dialogue hampers its creepiness.

There's also a couple of stylistic things; the "zero (0)" stuff is kinda pointless, and I feel like 'his' and the like in the containment procedures should be gender-neutral. On the other hand, the "eyes in the back of their mouth" is a really good line, and form some reason I like the idea of something necessitating a house 'set' to be built to contain it. There's good stuff in here, and you've structured and paced it well, it just doesn't really do it for me, I'm afraid.

Also, probably dumb question, is 3000-1 what's cancelling the edit at the end?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This isn't too bad, though I feel it suffers from a few similarities to other contest entries. I'm not sure the ending had much of an effect, and I'm sort of lost as to the precise origin, though it seems the Foundation is, too, so that's maybe not so bad. (I mean, I've never felt someone watching me in my life; I always assumed that was just literary convenience, like love at first sight.) Couple things:

Often times those who experience SCP-3000 tend to report it from ‘behind’. If their back is to the wall, wherever the nearest window or darkest corner in their sight is, they tend to avoid.

Doesn't make sense as worded.

it stole my light, but I have me! Cannot stop me now, fool. need weapon. combat them. destroy them.

Capitalization. "I have me"? I mean, he's distinctly delirious, just making sure that was what you meant to put there.

Novote, but I agree with others, this has great potential.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is stellar for an author's first post on the site (and for a major contest, at that; props to you) but I did hold some issue with a few parts. I honestly think that Buttfranklin covered those points fully (I second all of them) but I'd also like to add that a good half or so of Addendum SCP-3000-A felt like stuff that should have gone in the Description. No-vote for those reasons, but it's definitely far from 'bad'.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Don't feel the need to rebuild it from scratch! It's doing pretty well in terms of votes, and don't let the length of my comment fool you: I'm just one person, listing one person's set of criticisms.

I see you're hung up on logic. I get obsessed with whether my pieces make sense when put under a microscope too. We shouldn't be. Barely anyone notices if your writing contains some minor logical flaw. After all, how many movies become blockbuster successes with nine-figure profits despite being riddled with plot holes? If you're writing and come across a toss-up between logic and impact, err on the side of the latter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the feedback.

response
– hide block

You don't need to apologize about criticism, I'm pretty aware that dialogue isn't my strong suit. I have a sort of comfort zone when writing characters, and the D-Class & Captain were pretty far outside of it, so it felt sort of weak.

Beyond that, the grammatical stuff (using wrong words) mostly seems to come from the fact that I really never got enough criticism on this sort of writing from people experienced with it, since I always shy away from posting. I'll probably need to look a little closer at that on my next attempt.

Capitalization was a bit erratic, it's a stupid habit of mine I've been trying to break for years.

I guess for the whole [REDACTED] thing, it felt like it was an entity/anomaly that wasn't supposed to be defined by it's appearance. I get it, it's lazy writing, but I dunno. I have a crystal clear image of what they're supposed to look like (written down on a google doc with my other stuff), so it's not a cop-out at least. For the antisocial/sociopathic section, it was definitely more of a plot device. Antisocial was so I had a way to contain the entity without much risk of a reactive event, and sociopathic for the D-17729 log/last collapsible. It's not the best, but I'm not sure how to circumvent that.

Destruction of all memories was a bit unnecessary, but now it's kind of integral for the exploration log, and I can't quite figure out how to get rid of it without the exploration log falling apart. I always saw entities that destroy memories as a sort of self-preservation tactic to avoid being caught/detected. But it's definitely a bit of a throwaway, and in retrospect a couple of ideas like that seem to crop up in the piece.

The last addendum was a pure stream-of-consciousness, but I think that it might need a little work. I just don't know what would be best to change about it for the sake of impact without accidentally ruining it's tempo.

Thanks a bunch for the criticisms. I'm not sure how much I'm gonna tear up in the article yet (I tried to change the basic errors, but I'm pondering how I could amend the D-17729 section's prose.) but I'll try.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Typed this on my mobile with dodgy WiFi so I hope this gets through/doesn't have too many typos.

I downvoted, but I think you have promise. I'd like to see you write more for the site — you'll only improve with practice! You're good at the aspects of writing which are most difficult to learn.

+ critique
– In rough chronological order:

I don't see a good reason for the "dictionary definition" part of the containment procedures to exist. It's a very effective opening from a narrative standpoint, piquing the hell out of my interest; but given that "causal-absent paranoia" seems to be a Foundation-only term, I don't see why it should be mentioned in the public domain at all.

"Feeling of being watched" seems a bit cliché and cheap as a basis for a scip, but I suppose that's the jaded, desensitised SCP addict in me talking.

You've used a lot of "off-tone" language — stuff you wouldn't see in a scientific report. Examples I remember: "as of right now", "luring it in" (on this site, I strongly recommend against using "it" as a pronoun), "injection of a gaseous substance" (plain incorrect use of "injection"), "two things occur"; "adversity" means "a tough situation", and isn't a synonym for "an adverse reaction". And a few grammar errors/offences against my internal style guide, mainly capitalisation (e.g. after colons: what are you, Victorian, lmao, amirite?); also, I'd personally put a minus symbol in the header text of an expanded collapsible. i.e. "+ show" "– hide".

The antisocial/sociopath distinction didn't seem interesting or related to the themes of the underlying anomaly.

You've used a lot of redaction and "we don't know what it looks like". I can't help but see this as lazy writing; there's an infinite number of ways to combine words together to conjure up an image scarier than "we dunno, ooo". Let me put this in a different way: (usually) mystery is a nice breadcrumb trail to a meaty payoff, but it feels dissatisfying to nibble on breadcrumbs all the way through the piece.

The "destruction of all memories" feels tacked on. It doesn't mesh with the other ideas, mostly because you only have that one-off mention and don't develop it.

I'm going to be mean, so apologies in advance: that dialogue is cringeworthy. When was the last time you heard someone say "What is this, a horror movie?" in real life? When was the last time that you said to yourself "What the hell… My head…" when you thought you were completely on your own? You're expecting this guy to narrate every action he takes when you could easily have used some stage directions. A whispered "Fuck…" D-17729 winces and clutches his forehead is more realistic. That lowercase "kill them all" stuff is bad too. Imagine you were transcribing actual audio: what reason would you have to type the words you hear in lowercase?

That last D-class addendum kind of makes sense, but is a bit poorly explained.

Here's what I liked.

You obviously have a good idea of what effect your words will have on a reader. This seems a stupidly basic thing to say, what with it being the foundation of any kind of writing, but you read a lot of people's first attempts on this site and it's obvious they've fallen at that first hurdle. You have the ability to get out of your own head and into your reader's, which is a great skill for writing and hard to teach. I can't offer any specific lines as evidence for this, but it is evidenced by another thing I like:

You've structured the piece really well, one-off, undeveloped throwaway ideas aside. The paragraphs flow smoothly, and you've clearly thought about the order in which to present your information to keep the reader interested and unconfused. That highest-level, macro planning of fiction is hard, so well done for getting that down.

Finally, there are a few lines of flair which show you've got a good grasp of language. "eyes in the back of their mouth" was nice and spooky.

Final thoughts: I can tell you're influenced by the plain ol' creepypasta of this site's early days, which is both a blessing and a curse. On the downside, our current batch of SCP writers are more into the complex, thematic pieces that knowingly subvert tropes etc, so you're not going to get much kudos from them by keeping it old school. On the plus side, it's a welcome break from the artsy-fartsy stuff, and a lot of the audience of this site aren't writers. Also, I think you should think a little harder and more critically about the stuff you write, particularly in terms of whether it's too similar to stuff which has been written before, and whether any particular idea/section is strictly necessary or useful for what you've written.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it. +1

If I'm not mistaken, it's policy to use metric units for clinical tone, though, and you have a few spots where you used imperial.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The final collapsible got me good

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's a couple capitalization errors in both of the logs (near the end of the first and the beginning of the second), and a 3XXX in the last collapsible. That being said, I really liked this. Easily one of my favorites from this contest. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this a lot, it's really, really scary. Well done. That being said, there were a fair amount of copy-editing issues that could use a touch-up. I fixed a bunch of "it's" that should have been "its", for instance.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey. Here's my 3000 entry. Yeah, it's my first post (not my first foray into Foundation writing, but certainly my first time growing the balls to post something), and unfortunately, it was finished so late I didn't have much time to get it edited at all, so it's a cold post too. But I had an idea I really liked that fit the theme pretty well, so I just winged it, revised it like 100 times, and went with it. I know there's probably lots wrong with it. Please tear it apart, so I may learn from my mistakes. Also, lemme know if it feels too same-y to any other creatures. Beyond maybe SCP-966 (which I sort of tried to subvert) I haven't come across anything glaring, but I might've forgotten some. If it's practically identical to one, I'll remove it.

Rant over. Thanks for reading (and rating, if you did), and helping me grow as a writer.
